content.content:
[p.46]
46 
4. Start Your First Project 
 Overview 
This chapter describes how to create and run your first project. Before performing the instructions 
given in this chapter, please be sure to read all the instructions, gain a full understanding of the 
content of this manual, and correctly set the TM AI Cobot according to Chapters 2 and 3. 
 
 
IMPORTANT: 
Before starting using the robot, make sure to carry out all the initial tests and examinations 
listed below: 
 
1. The functional testing of Emergency Stop functions 
2. The functional testing of Safeguard functions 
3. The functional testing of Enabling Switch functions 
4. The functional testing of Force and Torque Limit functions 
5. The functional testing of Speed Limit functions 
6. The functional testing of Soft Axis Limit functions 
7. The functional testing of Safety Output functions 
 
The robot and the system should perform either Category 1 Stop or Category 2 Stop 
concerning these different safety functions. For details about the stop categories, trigger and 
resume method of the safety functions mentioned above, see the corresponding safety 
system version of the Safety Manual. 
 
Before starting the first project, make sure that the Safeguard for Human-Machine Safety 
Settings Input is OPEN and the robot is in the Human-Machine Safety Settings state (as 
happened when the Indication Light Ring of the End Module is flashing purple. For details, 
see the section of Safety Connection in corresponding hardware version of the Hardware 
Installation Manual. 
 
 Initial Setting 
When your device is connected to the TM AI Cobot for the first time, follow the steps to complete 
the following settings: 
 
Step 1. 
Follow the steps to set up the robot. 
Step 2. 
Select the interface language. 
Step 3. 
Set the system time. 
Step 4. 
Complete the Network settings. 
Step 5. 
Perform voice settings. 
 

[p.47]
47 
 M/A Mode and FreeBot 
Confirm the Operation Mode of the robot at this time. Check the MODE Indicator on the Robot 
Stick, and identify whether the lamp position is marked as M (MANUAL MODE) or A (AUTO MODE). 
The Operation Mode can also be identified by the Indication Light Ring of the End Module, 
where green light is MANUAL MODE, and the white light is AUTO MODE. If it is still in AUTO MODE, 
refer to the MODE switch function in Chapters 2 to switch to the MANUAL MODE to perform the 
follow-up operations of this chapter. When the MODE Indicator lamp position at M and the 
Indication Light Ring of the End Module is green, it is in MANUAL MODE. 
 
 
Figure 4 - 1: AUTO MODE / MANUAL MODE 
 
In MANUAL MODE, users can press the FREE button to hand guide the robot. The hand guiding 
function is limited to MANUAL MODE. 
 

[p.48]
48 
 
Figure 4 - 2: FREE Button 
 
 
IMPORTANT: 
Make sure the robot stays still, and no extra force applies to the external force sensor, such as 
touching the tool or the external force sensor by hand, before releasing the FREE button. If 
releasing the FREE button while the robot is moving, the robot might vibrate or hop for the 
brake, which may result in errors on joints in the extreme conditions. 
 
 Build and Run the First Project 
If this is the first time of unpacking the TM AI Cobot, there will be no project in the robot. Users can 
build the first project by the instructions given in this section.  
 
TMflow has two programming methods: Flow and Script. Users can choose either one of them as 
the first project. The flow is a graphical programming interface developed for TM AI Cobot. Users 
can start quickly and intuitively build a project by dragging the modularized nodes without any 
programming experience or writing additional codes. The script is a program editor for 
experienced users with writing scripts. Users can write programs of the robot motion logic with the 
rich tools and library provided in the script editor. 
 
The following project aims to run back and forth between two points (P1 and P2) as steps 
described below: 
 
 
Step 1 
Confirm the Operation Mode of the robot. If it is not in MANUAL MODE, use the 
MODE switch function to switch to MANUAL MODE. 
Step 2 
Navigate to â‰،, and click Project to enter the Project Editing Page. 
 
CAUTION:

import sys
sys.stdout.reconfigure(encoding='utf-8')

BASE = r'C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\DAEHYUN_PRIVATE\TM Robot'

with open(BASE + r'\tmflow_full_text.txt', encoding='utf-8') as f:
    content = f.read()

pages = content.split('\n\n===== PAGE ')

def get_pages(start, end):
    result = []
    for p in range(start, min(end+1, len(pages))):
        text = pages[p]
        lines = text.split('\n')
        clean = '\n'.join(lines[7:])
        result.append(f'[p.{p-1}]\n{clean}')
    return '\n'.join(result)

# ى„¹ى…کë³„ ي…چىٹ¤يٹ¸ ى¶”ى¶œ (PDF ë¬¸ى„œ يژکى‌´ى§€ ë²ˆيک¸ ê¸°ى¤€, PDF index = doc_page + 1)
sections = {
    'ch2_startup':        (26, 34),
    'ch3_safety':         (35, 46),
    'ch4_first_project':  (47, 57),
    'ch5_operation':      (58, 123),
    'ch6_point_base':     (124, 132),
    'ch7_create_base':    (132, 138),
    'ch8_tcp':            (138, 145),
    'ch9_motion':         (145, 166),
    'ch10_logic':         (166, 184),
    'ch11_ai_vision':     (184, 186),
    'ch12_comm':          (186, 200),
    'ch13_component':     (198, 201),
    'ch14_force':         (201, 232),
    'ch15_op_scene':      (231, 235),
    'ch16_comp_editor':   (235, 251),
    'ch17_collision':     (251, 254),
    'appendix_a':         (253, 258),
    'appendix_b':         (257, 276),
    'appendix_c_modbus':  (275, 292),
    'appendix_d_ethernet':(291, 297),
    'appendix_e_ethernetip':(296, 303),
    'appendix_f_profinet':(302, 309),
    'appendix_g_errors':  (308, 400),
}

for name, (start, end) in sections.items():
    text = get_pages(start, end)
    fname = BASE + f'\\sec_{name}.txt'
    with open(fname, 'w', encoding='utf-8') as f:
        f.write(text)
    print(f'{name} (p{start}-{end}): {len(text)} chars')

   343â†’Move Control:  
   344â†’Cartesian: 
   345â†’The robot moves by the Cartesian base in Base or Tool direction. 
   346â†’Joint: 
   347â†’The robot moves by the joint. 
   348â†’Align:

   357â†’In the Cartesian and the Joint tabs, there are two motion control methods: 
   358â†’ïپ® 
   359â†’Single-joint/single-axis movement 
   360â†’To use single joint/single-axis movement, click the joint/axis to move first, and 
   361â†’then hold the Enabling Switch on Robot Stick and press the âٹ• button or âٹ– 
   362â†’button on the Robot Stick to move the joint/axis in the positive or the opposite 
   363â†’direction. Press the STOP button to switch the selected joint/axis to the next 
   364â†’option. 
   365â†’ïپ® 
   366â†’Moving to a specific target 
   367â†’To move a specific target, enter the target in the textbox at right, hold the 
   368â†’Enabling Switch on Robot Stick, and press the PLAY button on the Robot Stick 
   369â†’to move the robot to the target position.

   418â†’Joystick 
   419â†’Joystick control is a convenient function for users to control the robot when 
   420â†’they are focusing on teaching or inconvenient to use FreeBot on an extended-
   421â†’length robot. In addition to motion control, joystick control provides the 
   422â†’functions of point recording and gripper control for users' convenience to 
   423â†’program. This function allows users to control the robot through Joystick in 
   424â†’manual mode only. 
   425â†’ 
   426â†’Before using it, users should be aware of the limitation and definitions below. 
   427â†’1. 
   428â†’To comply with the single point of control in the safety regulations, only 
   429â†’one device, either Robot Stick or Joystick, is available at a time. 
   430â†’2. 
   431â†’Joystick supports the XInput type devices only. 
   432â†’3. 
   433â†’If multiple joysticks are connected, only the first recognized is available. 
   434â†’4. 
   435â†’Joystick control is only available in Project and certain pages with 
   436â†’controller functions.

   595â†’Quick Control 
   596â†’The Quick Control  
   597â†’ is a floating window with a simplified motion control interface. 
   598â†’Users can access the flow editing area with Quick Control opened, allowing 
   599â†’simultaneous jogging and project editing and removing the need to repeatedly open 
   600â†’and close the controller page. It brings convenience when users have trouble access to 
   601â†’screens, mice, or keyboards. 
   602â†’ 
   603â†’The Quick Control comes with the control type button, DoF buttons (linear/rotation), 
   604â†’and Freebot Lock buttons (linear/rotation) at the top.

   953â†’Point Manager 
   954â†’Point Manager lists all points and their parameters including the category of points: 
   955â†’General point, fine-tuning point, dynamic point, the reference Base to which the point 
   956â†’is attached, and the tools used by the point. For the creation and applicable nodes of all 
   957â†’categories of points, refer to the Point node and Touch Stop node. In the Point Manager, 
   958â†’ represents Vision Base, and 
   959â†’ represents Custom Base. Click the pencil icon at 
   960â†’the left side of the point to go to the information page of the point where users can 
   961â†’modify the point name and find out the reference coordinates, tools, and detail 
   962â†’coordinates of the point: [X, Y, Z, Rx, Ry, Rz]. Users can set the type of motion with either 
   963â†’PTP or Line and its speed at the bottom.

   971â†’flow. For example, if Point["P1"].Value functions in the flow, no exclamation mark will 
   972â†’present since it regards P1 as used in the flow. However, if written as Point[var_A].Value

  1026â†’Project Variables 
  1027â†’TMflow comes with its variable structure in list format for users to identify status, data 
  1028â†’types, names, and values of variables or arrays. After choosing the data type and 
  1029â†’
  1030â†’[p.81]
  1031â†’81 
  1032â†’assigning the value and the name, users can click the Add button to add new variables 
  1033â†’or arrays. Click the funnel icon to filter items in the list with the data type. The items in 
  1034â†’the list are in the order of the dates to create. The newer the date is, the upper in the list 
  1035â†’it is. Users can change the list order by clicking Type or Name. 
  1036â†’ 
  1037â†’In addition, users can use Import/Export to read text files as entities, and users can 
  1038â†’break them down into pieces or trace them back to the plain code with programming. 
  1039â†’The text file to read must be less than 2MB in the assigned path. Users can use Text File 
  1040â†’Manager in Configuration to check text files in the list. Variables created with the type 
  1041â†’of string can read data in the text files as values. Array, Global Variables, and Variables 
  1042â†’created with other types are not supported. 
  1043â†’ 
  1044â†’ 
  1045â†’IMPORTANT: 
  1046â†’ïپ¬ 
  1047â†’Use " " to enclose the string when inputting the string value to avoid 
  1048â†’being treated as a variable. 
  1049â†’ïپ¬ 
  1050â†’Do not use reserved words such as var in naming. 
  1051â†’ 
  1052â†’Global Variable  
  1053â†’Using global variables is similar to the variable system in the project, and global 
  1054â†’variables in this system apply to all projects in addition. Refer to 10.2.2 Global Variables 
  1055â†’for details.

  1057â†’Display 
  1058â†’In Display, users can set the variables to be displayed on the display panel and interact 
  1059â†’with users when the project is running. Variables are divided into two types: displayed to 
  1060â†’users and input by users. The page where users may input variable value can be 
  1061â†’protected with a password, to avoid unauthorized operators intervening with or 
  1062â†’modifying the robot's motion behaviors by modifying the variables. On the top part of 
  1063â†’the display management panel, the time period of the refreshing of the display of 
  1064â†’variables can be selected from 300, 500, or 1000 in مژ³. The variable will update the 
  1065â†’display information according to the set time. Set the refresh time appropriately to avoid 
  1066â†’users receiving wrong variable information.

  1095â†’click New to add a stop watch. Stop Watch includes four parts, the beginning node, the 
  1096â†’ending node, records in a specific variable, and the note description. Check the bullet 
  1097â†’before Start or End. Then, click the note to be configured to complete the configuration. 
  1098â†’To save the variable, when Stop Watch is running, the time result obtained while 
  1099â†’running can be output as the variable to help users analyze this parameter. Select a

  1199â†’Joint Loading can monitor the loading that every node brings to the robot joints and

1200â†’keep repeating peak torque away. Simply click the icon of Joint Loading to use the 
  1201â†’function. 
  1202â†’ 
  1203â†’Follow the steps below to use Joint Loading: 
  1204â†’1. 
  1205â†’Click the switch next to Display Indicator to turn on or off the rectangle encloses 
  1206â†’each node in the project. 
  1207â†’2. 
  1208â†’Check or uncheck the desired levels of the risk indication on each node. 
  1209â†’3. 
  1210â†’Click the Apply button to have the setting take effect. 
  1211â†’ 
  1212â†’Once Display Indicator turned on and all levels of the risk checked, the rectangle 
  1213â†’encloses each node in yellow for High Risk, blue for Low Risk, and gray for Unknown. 
  1214â†’To turn off the rectangle encloses each node, switch off Display Indicator in Joint 
  1215â†’Loading. 
  1216â†’ 
  1217â†’Gray (unknown) denotes the system did not execute this part of motion during the last 
  1218â†’operation, and thus the system cannot figure the output of each joint during the actual 
  1219â†’process. Yellow (high risk) denotes the output at some joints is higher than the allowable 
  1220â†’repetitive peak torque. The condition of the output, in the long run, will considerably 
  1221â†’affect the service life of the joint. Users can increase the joint acceleration time or 
  1222â†’decrease the joint motion speed to reduce the joint unit loading. 
  1223â†’ 
  1224â†’Below the Apply button, every node in the project will be listed with the type and the 
  1225â†’name. Joint Loading on every node takes effect after the project execution. The high 
  1226â†’risk node will come with the reference speed reduction ration in the list. Users can click 
  1227â†’the reverse alphabetic, alphabetic, or chronologic buttons to sort the listed nodes.

  1397â†’Step Run 
  1398â†’Step Run is used to confirm accuracy of the edited motion. The first node of a Step Run 
  1399â†’can be a Start, a Point Node, or any node that is not grayed out. This allows users to 
  1400â†’easily evaluate the correctness of the node/motion. Step Run can start running from the 
  1401â†’selected node to have the robot moving by pressing and holding the Enabling Switch 
  1402â†’and the Play button on the Robot Stick. At any time, releasing the Enabling Switch and 
  1403â†’or the Play button on the Robot Stick will stop the robot movement, and pressing and 
  1404â†’holding the Enabling Switch and or the Play button on the Robot Stick again will start 
  1405â†’the robot moving from where it stopped. When the Step Run pane displays (Node 
  1406â†’name)_finish, the node running is completed. Release the Enabling Switch and the Play 
  1407â†’button on the Robot Stick and press and hold again to continue to the next node. If the 
  1408â†’Step Run window is open, the FREE button at the End Module cannot be used to hand 
  1409â†’guide the robot. Also, both the variable system and the decision formula will not 
  1410â†’operate. When there is a logical branch node (e.g., If Node, Gateway Node) the path of 
  1411â†’pass or fail can be selected freely to check that each decision branchâ€™s internal motion 
  1412â†’programming is correct using Step Run. Click 
  1413â†’ to launch 3D Viewer.

  1733â†’ Connection Settings 
  1734â†’In Connection Settings, users can set Modbus Slave related settings, TM Ethernet 
  1735â†’Slave, Profinet Server, and EtherNetIP Server Make sure the card does secure to the 
  1736â†’designated slot and the cable does connect to the appropriate port before setting items 
  1737â†’in Connection. 
  1738â†’ 
  1739â†’For Modbus Slave, the system provides two Modbus communication methods: Modbus 
  1740â†’TCP and Modbus RTU. Click Disable/Enable switch icon on the top to turn on/off the 
  1741â†’mode. Once TCP is turned on, the system works as a Modbus TCP server for user 
  1742â†’configured clients with IP filtered and permissions to access robot data. If RTU is turned 
  1743â†’on, it is possible to access robot data with configurations via serial connections. Click the 
  1744â†’Code Table button in the bottom left to open the Modbus slave encoding definition file. 
  1745â†’ 
  1746â†’To use TM Ethernet Slave, click Disable/Enable switch icon on the top to turn on/off the 
  1747â†’mode. Once turned on, the system works as a socket server for users to configure clients 
  1748â†’with IP filtered and permissions to access robot data. TM Ethernet Slave follows the 
  1749â†’protocol introduced in Programming Language TMscript. 
  1750â†’ 
  1751â†’To use Profinet Server or EtherNetIP Server, click Disable/Enable switch icon on the 
  1752â†’top to turn on/off the mode. Once turned on, the system works as a Profinet Server or 
  1753â†’EtherNetIP Server for robot data accessing. For Endianness, users can click on the 
  1754â†’respective bullet to place the most significant byte first and the least significant byte last 
  1755â†’with Big-endian or the opposite with Little-endian. To check the data table, click the 
  1756â†’Code Table button. 
  1757â†’ 
  1758â†’To use the Profinet Server or EtherNetIP Server functions in Expression Editor Setting: 
  1759â†’1. 
  1760â†’Click Expression Editor > Connection, and select Profinet or EtherNetIP in the 
  1761â†’menu. 
  1762â†’2. 
  1763â†’Select the desired item in the Function dropdown. 
  1764â†’ 
  1765â†’
  1766â†’[p.102]
  1767â†’102 
  1768â†’ 
  1769â†’NOTE: 
  1770â†’When turning on Profinet Server or EtherNetIP Server, if a message prompts users 
  1771â†’ïپ¬ 
  1772â†’for the listed field bus is enabled, please disable the current activated field bus 
  1773â†’before changing the setting. 
  1774â†’ïپ¬ 
  1775â†’for rebooting the robot, please power cycle the robot to change the firmware of 
  1776â†’the field bus device and manually enable the target field bus in the setting 
  1777â†’again. 
  1778â†’ïپ¬ 
  1779â†’for failed to activate device, please check the device and the driver are both 
  1780â†’installed correctly.

  1782â†’ External Device 
  1783â†’In External Device, users can configure and administer the connecting EtherCAT IO 
  1784â†’external devices. The device list will display the connecting EtherCAT IO external devices 
  1785â†’when the system is on. A device must connect to the control box before the system is on 
  1786â†’to be on the list. If the device is a TM Plug&Play EtherCAT external expansion module, it 
  1787â†’will not show in the device list since it is plug-and-play without configuration required. 
  1788â†’Users can click Manage Known Device to view and delete the current configured 
  1789â†’external devices, and click Manage ESI Files to view and delete the imported ESI files. 
  1790â†’The ESI file is mainly for replacing the possibility of connection failure caused by the 
  1791â†’outdated ESI file in the device.

  1798â†’Vendor ID: The device manufacturer information. Users can look up the 
  1799â†’manufacturerâ€™s name on the EtherCAT official website.

  1804â†’Device Type: Select the function type of the external device. 
  1805â†’ïپ® 
  1806â†’Digital Value Range: If the external device goes by the IO function type, users must 
  1807â†’configure the associated upper and lower bounds for the digital-analog conversion 
  1808â†’to map the digital values to the associated analog ones accurately ( -10V ~ 10V ). 
  1809â†’ïپ® 
  1810â†’Function: If the external device goes by the IO function type, users must select the 
  1811â†’IO function type (DI, DO, AI, AO) to add when adding a configured data index. 
  1812â†’ïپ® 
  1813â†’Channel: The amount of IO channels will generate automatically by the configured 
  1814â†’index. 
  1815â†’ïپ® 
  1816â†’Index: Users must select the index associated with the data by the description 
  1817â†’document provided by the external device. 
  1818â†’ 
  1819â†’After configuring the items above, click Add Setting to add an entry of the setting in the 
  1820â†’table below. To edit or remove an entry, click the associated edit button. 
  1821â†’ 
  1822â†’Finally, after finishing the external device configuration, click Save to save the settings, 
  1823â†’so the addition or modification can take effect after the robot restarts mandatorily.

  1830â†’modules. 
  1831â†’ïپ¬ 
  1832â†’Each analog index is limited to 1 channel.

  1893â†’ IO Setup

  1895â†’and the meaning represented by the User-Defined IO can be set. Using User-Defined IO, 
  1896â†’users can trigger or read the button on the Robot Stick with an external device through 
  1897â†’the IO port on the Control Box. Users can also change the serial port configuration on 
  1898â†’Control Box and End Module in Serial Port. If users wish to name certain IOs, then can 
  1899â†’do so in Custom IO Name to give names to the IOs. After the setting is complete, click the 
  1900â†’Save button at bottom right to save the setting.

  1993â†’ Motion Settings 
  1994â†’In Motion Settings, users can set Speed Suppression to have the robot adjust its rate of 
  1995â†’motion by the custom-values of the Safety Tool Speed Limit and the Joint Speed Limit. 
  1996â†’Refer to the formula of Target Speed to input the values in the fields next to A,B and 
  1997â†’C,D, and click SAVE when done. 
  1998â†’ 
  1999â†’ 
  2000â†’NOTE: 
  2001â†’HW 3.2 does not support the joint speed suppression function. 
  2002â†’ 
  2003â†’The robot goes with a varying scale of speed fluctuations at different settings of motion 
  2004â†’speed. The speed fluctuation, in general, will be minor in low motion speed and massive 
  2005â†’in high motion speed. 
  2006â†’ 
  2007â†’ïپ¬ 
  2008â†’A,C stands for the tolerance value of the speed fluctuation in the low Safety Tool 
  2009â†’Speed Limit or the Joint Speed Limit while the robot is in low motion speed. 
  2010â†’ïپ¬ 
  2011â†’B,D stands for the tolerance ratio of the speed fluctuation in the high Safety Tool 
  2012â†’Speed Limit or the Joint Speed Limit while the robot is in high motion speed. 
  2013â†’ 
  2014â†’ 
  2015â†’NOTE: 
  2016â†’To avoid setting the speed off the limit, value A,C adapts better in the low Safety Tool 
  2017â†’Speed Limit or Joint Speed Limit, and value B,D in the high Safety Tool Speed Limit 
  2018â†’or Joint Speed Limit. 
  2019â†’
  2020â†’[p.108]
  2021â†’108 
  2022â†’ 
  2023â†’ 
  2024â†’The custom-values of Safety Tool Speed Limit and the Joint Speed Limit refer to 
  2025â†’Configuration>Safety> Speed & Force>Performance Safety and Configuration 
  2026â†’>Safety> Speed & Force>Human-Machine Safety for the normal and the collaborative 
  2027â†’operation, respectively.  
  2028â†’ 
  2029â†’ 
  2030â†’NOTE: 
  2031â†’The Speed Suppression function is always enabled. The system will adjust the 
  2032â†’motion speed of nodes automatically to the limit value of Safety Tool Speed and 
  2033â†’Joint Speed set by users on the Safety Settings page. These nodes include Point 
  2034â†’Node, Circle Node, Pallet Node, Move Node, Touch Stop Node (Line), Vision Node 
  2035â†’(Fixed Point), Vision Node (Servoing), and Path Node. 
  2036â†’

  2038â†’Settings function is triggered. When the robot enters the collaborative operation or the 
  2039â†’collaborative workspace, the system achieves the speed setting in the set time value.

  2128â†’ Import/Export 
  2129â†’In Import/Export, users can import items from the flash drive or export items to the 
  2130â†’flash drive. The label of the flash drive must be TMROBOT. Insert the flash drive to the 
  2131â†’Control Box before using the function. 
  2132â†’ 
  2133â†’ïپ¬ 
  2134â†’To use the Export function:  
  2135â†’Click the Export button at the top left, and then select the desired file in the Select 
  2136â†’file box. Click the item in this box to add the item to the Selected files box. After 
  2137â†’completing the new addition, click Export at the bottom right to start the Export 
  2138â†’procedure. 
  2139â†’ïپ¬ 
  2140â†’To use the Import function: 
  2141â†’Click on the Import button at the top left, select the robot of the data source in the 
  2142â†’flash drive from the robot list, and then select the desired data from the Select file 
  2143â†’box. Click an item in this box to add the item to the Selected files box. After 
  2144â†’completing the new addition, click Import at the bottom right to start the Import 
  2145â†’procedure. 
  2146â†’ 
  2147â†’
  2148â†’[p.111]
  2149â†’111 
  2150â†’ 
  2151â†’NOTE: 
  2152â†’While using Import/Export, if there are duplicated Project files in the Selected

     3â†’9. Motion Programming 
     4â†’ Overview 
     5â†’This chapter will introduce the robot's commonly used motion nodes, describe its basic features 
     6â†’and motion modes, and help users understand blending. 
     7â†’ 
     8â†’PTP 
     9â†’Line 
    10â†’Figure 9 - 1: TM AI Cobot Motion Modes 
    11â†’ 
    12â†’ïپ¬ 
    13â†’PTP (Point to Point): The robot moves to the target point along the closest path of the 
    14â†’joint angle space 
    15â†’ïپ¬ 
    16â†’Line: The tool moves in a straight line at the specified speed 
    17â†’ 
    18â†’ 
    19â†’NOTE: 
    20â†’1. 
    21â†’User can set PTP and Line speed via variables. However, the motion of the point 
    22â†’node comes with the issues of mixing trajectories because the point node must 
    23â†’calculate the motion commands in advance. Accordingly, if the speed variable of 
    24â†’the point node is changed in other threads, the speed of the robot will not be the 
    25â†’current value of the variable. To ensure the correctness of the speed variable in the 
    26â†’motion command with the point node, users need to add a Wait For node or 
    27â†’update the speed variable ahead of the point node in the same flow. 
    28â†’2. 
    29â†’To maintain the smoothness and the continuity of the motion, the flow in process 
    30â†’calculates and sets each value in the nodes with the logic and the sequence of the 
    31â†’programming. The calculation is made much earlier than the time to actually 
    32â†’perform the motion. If using variables as the parameters in the motion nodes, 
    33â†’users must insert other nodes such as the Set nodes, the Network nodes, or the 
    34â†’Listen nodes before the motion node to assign the correct values to the variables. 
    35â†’
    36â†’[p.145]
    37â†’145 
    38â†’ 
    39â†’The following will introduce motion programming through a flow project. 
    40â†’ 
    41â†’ Point to Point (PTP) 
    42â†’ PTP is the Fastest Way to Move 
    43â†’The PTP mode determines the robotâ€™s motion by calculating the angular variation of each 
    44â†’axis, and is not limited by the singularity point. If not limited to the robotâ€™s motion path, 
    45â†’such as the initial point of the project, it is favored to select PTP to ensure no problems will 
    46â†’occur when moving this point with various poses. 
    47â†’ 
    48â†’IMPORTANT: 
    49â†’Singular points are described in the Safety Manual. 
    50â†’ 
    51â†’ 
    52â†’Figure 9 - 2: PTP Motion 
    53â†’ 
    54â†’ Speed of PTP Motion 
    55â†’The PTP speed is based on the motion joint that takes the longest time. The PTP mode may 
    56â†’cause the TCP to exceed speed limit, especially when the robot length is longer, and should 
    57â†’be avoided. In PTP speed setting, users can set speed percentage and time to top speed. 
    58â†’ 
    59â†’ 
    60â†’Figure 9 - 3: Speed of PTP Motion 
    61â†’
    62â†’[p.146]
    63â†’146 
    64â†’ 
    65â†’ Plan for PTP Movement 
    66â†’In the example, working with the TCP T4 to move the workpiece from P1 to P2 does not need 
    67â†’to limit the robot movement path, using the PTP setting at the P2 Point node, after the robot 
    68â†’reaches P1. In this case after the arm reaches P1, the fastest movement path will be planned 
    69â†’to move to P2. 
    70â†’ 
    71â†’ 
    72â†’Figure 9 - 4: PTP Application Examples 
    73â†’ 
    74â†’ PTP Smart Pose Choosing 
    75â†’By default, in PTP motion, the system will choose the configuration determined from the 
    76â†’recorded robot pose and move to the target point. This feature has the system ignore the 
    77â†’configuration from recorded robot pose and choose the most efficient configuration on the 
    78â†’way to the goal. This feature is applicable to Vision, Point, and Path. However, it is not 
    79â†’applicable to Move. 
    80â†’ 
    81â†’ 
    82â†’Figure 9 - 5: PTP Smart Pose Choosing 
    83â†’ 
    84â†’
    85â†’[p.147]
    86â†’147 
    87â†’ Line 
    88â†’ Line Moves the Shortest Distance 
    89â†’A straight line is the shortest distance 
    90â†’between two points. The Line mode 
    91â†’specifies that the path between the two 
    92â†’points is planned as a straight line. 
    93â†’ 
    94â†’Figure 9 - 6: Line Motion 
    95â†’ Speed of Line Motion 
    96â†’ 
    97â†’Line mode may cause joint speeding. Try 
    98â†’to avoid speeding close to a singularity 
    99â†’point, or make the posture large-angle 
   100â†’movements over a short distance. 
   101â†’ 
   102â†’Figure 9 - 7: Speed of Line 
   103â†’ 
   104â†’Percentage of Typical Speed and absolute speed value can be set in Line speed setting. 
   105â†’The available range of ABS Speed is from 0 to 4500 مژœ/s, and the available ABS Time to top 
   106â†’speed is from 150 to 9999 مژ³. Check the box next to Link to project speed to align the speed 
   107â†’with the project speed. Once selected ABS, users can check Constant TCP speed to ensure 
   108â†’TCP speed remains constant at the ABS Speed. 
   109â†’ 
   110â†’ 
   111â†’Figure 9 - 8: Link to Project Speed 
   112â†’
   113â†’[p.148]
   114â†’148 
   115â†’ 
   116â†’The typical speed is the suggested maximum speed of a regular application, and is the 
   117â†’linear speed of the center of the tool flange of the robot, used in the specified cycles defined 
   118â†’in these specifications: 
   119â†’ïپ¬ 
   120â†’Repeatability 
   121â†’ïپ¬ 
   122â†’Maximum Payload 
   123â†’ 
   124â†’If users want to set higher speed, use the ABS setting in the node. The maximum speed of 
   125â†’the robot is highly dependent on the pose of the robot and joint motion. Refer to the 
   126â†’maximum joint speed in the specification for the composition of velocities of the tool end. 
   127â†’ 
   128â†’  Plan for Line Movement 
   129â†’The figure below explains that this 
   130â†’project sets two points P1, P2, and 
   131â†’tool T22. Using the Line setting at the 
   132â†’P2 Point node, after the arm reaches 
   133â†’P1, it will move to P2 with Line path. 
   134â†’ 
   135â†’ 
   136â†’Figure 9 - 9: Line Application Example 
   137â†’ 
   138â†’ 
   139â†’IMPORTANT: 
   140â†’ABS (Absolute) speed setting, including Point, Path, Move, and nodes can be used when 
   141â†’the speed is linked with Project Speed. When not clicked, the arm motion maintains the 
   142â†’ABS set speed. The warning window will pop up when the speed setting exceeds 250 مژœ/s, 
   143â†’and check automatically, display "Speed exceeds 250 مژœ/s, needs to be linked with 
   144â†’Project Speed ". 
   145â†’ 
   146â†’ Blending 
   147â†’ Blending in Movement 
   148â†’In the process of planning a 
   149â†’trajectory, the robot will not exactly 
   150â†’pass through each programmed 
   151â†’point, which has the advantages of 
   152â†’reducing the number of robotic 
   153â†’ 
   154â†’Figure 9 - 10: Blending in Space 
   155â†’
   156â†’[p.149]
   157â†’149 
   158â†’brakes and reducing wear and 
   159â†’shortening the cycle time. As shown 
   160â†’in the figure below, a movement from

   651â†’ Pallet Node 
   652â†’This node can set three-point coordinates and the values of row and column to control the 
   653â†’robotâ€™s motion between the rows and the columns. There are a total of two modes, 
   654â†’applicable to regular display applications, such as: pallet placement applications.

   657â†’Pallet Pattern: Set the pallet pattern that goes from the left or the right line by line with 
   658â†’or without carriage returns. 
   659â†’ïپ¬ 
   660â†’3 points to construct this pallet: The 1st Point is the start point of the 1st row and 
   661â†’determines the robot posture. The 2nd Point is the end point of the 1st row, and the 3rd 
   662â†’Point is the end point of the last row. 
   663â†’ïپ¬ 
   664â†’Apporach Point: Establish the approach point by referencing the 1st, 2nd, or 3rd point. 
   665â†’Once set, the same reference will apply to each pallet point's front. The robot will go to 
   666â†’the approach point and move to the pallet point with the LINE motion mode. 
   667â†’ïپ¬ 
   668â†’Number of Rows and Columns: Define the number rows and columns.

   715â†’ Listen Node 
   716â†’In the Listen Node, a socket server can be established and be connected by an external 
   717â†’device to communicate according to the defined protocol. All the functions available in 
   718â†’Expression Editor can also be executed in Listen Node. 
   719â†’ 
   720â†’
   721â†’[p.163]
   722â†’163 
   723â†’ 
   724â†’Figure 9 - 27: Listen Node 
   725â†’ 
   726â†’ïپ¬ 
   727â†’Send Message: 
   728â†’When entering this node, it will initiate a message 
   729â†’ïپ¬ 
   730â†’Print Received Data in Log: Enable Communication Log (shown on the right) 
   731â†’ïپ¬ 
   732â†’Connection Timeout: When entering this node, if more than the Connection Timeout 
   733â†’(milliseconds) passes without connecting, it will timeout. If <= 0, no timeout 
   734â†’ïپ¬ 
   735â†’Data Timeout: When connected, the timeout will be exceeded if no communication 
   736â†’packet arrives in Data Timeout مژ³. If <= 0, no timeout 
   737â†’ 
   738â†’Socket Server is set up after the project is running and closed after the project is stopped. 
   739â†’When the Socket Server is successfully established, the IP and Port will be displayed in the 
   740â†’Notice Log window on the right. 
   741â†’ 
   742â†’IP  
   743â†’Human-Machine Interface ïƒ  System ïƒ  Network ïƒ  IP Address 
   744â†’Port 
   745â†’5890 
   746â†’ 
   747â†’When the process enters the Listen Node, it stays in the Listen Node until it triggers and 
   748â†’leaves with the exit condition. 
   749â†’ 
   750â†’Pass: 
   751â†’Executes ScriptExit() or item stopped 
   752â†’Fail: 
   753â†’1. 
   754â†’Connection Timeout 
   755â†’2. 
   756â†’Data Timeout  
   757â†’3. 
   758â†’Before the Socket Server been established successfully, the flow process has 
   759â†’entered the Listen Node 
   760â†’
   761â†’[p.164]
   762â†’164 
   763â†’ 
   764â†’The commands received by the listen node will be executed in order. If the command is not 
   765â†’valid, an error message will be returned carrying the line number with errors. If the 
   766â†’command is valid, it will be executed. 
   767â†’ 
   768â†’The commands can be divided into two categories. The first category is commands which 
   769â†’can be accomplished in instance, like assigning variable value. The second category is 
   770â†’commands that need to be executed in sequence, like motion commands and IO value 
   771â†’assignment. The second category command will be placed in the queue and executed in 
   772â†’order. Refer to Programming Language TMscript for details on commands and 
   773â†’communication format. 
   774â†’ 
   775â†’

   779â†’10. Logic Programming 
   780â†’ Overview 
   781â†’This Chapter will introduce the logic nodes commonly used in the flow project, explain its basic 
   782â†’features and use methods, and let users understand how to let robots understand instructions and 
   783â†’commands and determine the next motion. In the area of logic programming, the most 
   784â†’IMPORTANT aspect is the application of variables. In TMflow, variables are mainly divided into 
   785â†’two categories: Global Variables and Local Variables, therefore, this chapter will introduce 
   786â†’variables first and explain how to assign them. It then covers basic explanations and examples of 
   787â†’how the logic nodes are paired with these variables. The following will introduce logic 
   788â†’programming through a flow project. 
   789â†’ 
   790â†’ Variable System 
   791â†’ Local Variables 
   792â†’Local Variables can only be called in a single project, and their effective range is only within 
   793â†’the project that created these variables. Variables are created through the Variable tab, at 
   794â†’the top of the TMflow interface. In this page, a single variable or array variable can be 
   795â†’declared and assigned a value. According to the different data formats, TMflow provides six 
   796â†’types of variables: int, float, string, double, bool, and byte. The meaning of the variables is 
   797â†’shown in the table below. If no value is assigned, a string initial value of empty, and the 
   798â†’remaining variables default to 0. The newly added local variables will appear in the project's 
   799â†’variable system, and begin with "var_" to represent local variables in the variable system. 
   800â†’Users can use dropdown to filter available variables in the list, and sort variables in the list 
   801â†’with the buttons of reverse alphabetical, alphabetical, or chronological. Click the Batch 
   802â†’Delete button to select multiple variables to delete. The exclamation mark denotes unused 
   803â†’in the flow. 
   804â†’

   115â†’ Start Node 
   116â†’In this node, users can view and set the initial state of the current project such as the initial 
   117â†’project running speed in Manual mode (the initial project running speed in Manual Mode is 
   118â†’fixed at 5% by default). Also, users can reset the states of DO and AO at the beginning of the 
   119â†’project to initialize DO and AO before the project starts. Check Enable continuous motion 
   120â†’to prevent the IO writing from interrupting the robot moving along the path of points for a 
   121â†’smooth process of moving. Enable continuous motion is unchecked by default. Check 
   122â†’Enable busy loop optimization to keep from intensive uses of CPU due to the busy loops. 
   123â†’ 
   124â†’Note: 
   125â†’More than 20 threads of execution will overload the system. There is no way to reduce 
   126â†’the loading even if checked Enable busy loop optimization.

   250â†’ WaitFor Node 
   251â†’The main function of the WaitFor Node is to hold the project, and continue to run after the 
   252â†’set conditions are met. It can be set according to IO, Time, Variables and other conditions to 
   253â†’judge whether to resume the run. 
   254â†’ 
   255â†’ Gateway Node 
   256â†’The Gateway node is a conditional judgment formula which is similar to IF Node. Instead of 
   257â†’output as YES or NO (IF Node), the Gateway node has a corresponding number of sub-
   258â†’nodes called CASE. When the project flow reaches a Gateway node, CASE would be judged 
   259â†’from the list in Case Settings with a top-down approach. Users can use the arrows up and 
   260â†’down to reorder the cases. If any condition is met, the project flow would continue from the 
   261â†’output of that CASE sub-node, and the judgment of the rest of the CASEs are skipped.  
   262â†’ 
   263â†’ 
   264â†’Figure 10 - 11: Gateway Node Judges Five Conditions 
   265â†’ 
   266â†’As shown above, there are 4 conditioned CASEs (and 1 Default case) which is relevant to 
   267â†’using 4 IF Nodes as shown in the figure below. From the view point of simplicity, Gateway 
   268â†’
   269â†’[p.174]
   270â†’174 
   271â†’nodes can simplify the layout and increase visibility of the flow. 
   272â†’ 
   273â†’ 
   274â†’Figure 10 - 12: IF Node Judges Four Conditions 
   275â†’ 
   276â†’ 
   277â†’NOTE: 
   278â†’If no conditions are matched, the project flow would remain deadlocked at the 
   279â†’Gateway node. Therefore, it is necessary to have a default case so that the project 
   280â†’flow can continue which is practical by leaving the last case sub-node with no 
   281â†’condition 
   282â†’ 
   283â†’ M-Decision Node 
   284â†’The M-Decision node comes with respective numbers of sub-nodes called CASE similar to 
   285â†’Gateway Node. When the project flow reaches a M-Decision node, the flow pauses and 
   286â†’prompts users to decide the CASE condition. Once decided, the project flow would continue 
   287â†’from the output of that CASE sub-node. 
   288â†’ 
   289â†’ 
   290â†’NOTE: 
   291â†’ïپ¬ 
   292â†’When the flow reaches the M-Decision node: 
   293â†’1. 
   294â†’Users cannot change the project speed. 
   295â†’2. 
   296â†’The message box prompts users making decisions in the view page, and 
   297â†’only those who get control of the system can make decisions. 
   298â†’ïپ¬ 
   299â†’M-Decision node is applicable to the main project flow, subflow, and thread.

   301â†’ Script Node 
   302â†’The Script Node allows users to rapidly and efficiently implement sophisticated logic and 
   303â†’computations into a TMflow program using the scripting language. Refer to Programming 
   304â†’Language TMscript for details on script programming.

   312â†’Users can regard script nodes as listen nodes with no external device required.  
   313â†’ïپ¬ 
   314â†’Since equivalent to a function, Script nodes are unavailable to define new 
   315â†’functions. 
   316â†’ïپ¬ 
   317â†’If using a script node in a thread, the motion-related functions are 
   318â†’unavailable.

   320â†’ Process  
   321â†’ Process Nodes 
   322â†’Flow nodes are mainly divided into four major categories: Pause, Stop, Goto, and Warp. The 
   323â†’function of the Pause node is the same as the pause on the Robot Stick. If the project is 
   324â†’running, and passes the Pause node, the project is paused. The Robot Stick can be used to 
   325â†’override the Pause, so the project continues to run. Users can use voice function in a Pause 
   326â†’node. The robot will read out the content when reaches the node. 
   327â†’ 
   328â†’The function of the Stop Node is the same as the Stop Button on the Robot Stick, as shown 
   329â†’in the figure below. If the project is running, and passes the Stop Node, the project is ended. 
   330â†’No node can be connected after Stop. If a Stop node does not exist in the flow, the project 
   331â†’will not end automatically. It is necessary to press the Stop Button on the Robot Stick to 
   332â†’end the project. 
   333â†’ 
   334â†’ 
   335â†’Figure 10 - 13: Stop Node Ends Project 
   336â†’ 
   337â†’The Goto Node provides users with unconditional transfer in the TMflow. When passing 
   338â†’through this Node, it will directly transfer to the set target Node, as shown in the figure, to 
   339â†’use the Goto Node. If the condition of the judgment formula is met, the next step transfers 
   340â†’to P1 directly. Although the application of Goto can be achieved using the connection 
   341â†’method, the complexity of the line will reduce the readability of the flow. The Goto Node will 
   342â†’
   343â†’[p.176]
   344â†’176 
   345â†’display the connection path only when the node is clicked, and the path of the connection 
   346â†’will be displayed and indicated by red lines. 
   347â†’ 
   348â†’ 
   349â†’Figure 10 - 14: Goto Node Flow Transfer 
   350â†’ 
   351â†’ 
   352â†’Figure 10 - 15: Goto Node Connection 
   353â†’ 
   354â†’The Warp Node transfers control the flow to another project and proceeds to run the target 
   355â†’project. Nodes in the original project that occur after the Warp are not executed. The 
   356â†’parameters of Variables, Base, and Tools will not pass to another project. If users want to 
   357â†’transfer Variables between two projects, Global Variables can be used. As shown in the 
   358â†’figure, when the TMflow of project runs beyond the Warp Node, the execution is transferred 
   359â†’to another project. 
   360â†’ 
   361â†’

   363â†’177

   366â†’ 
   367â†’Since the Warp node applies to transfer the flow between projects, the time consumed to 
   368â†’warp between projects varies from the mechanism for the initiations and the terminations 
   369â†’of the projects. To run the Warp node at its best efficiency, mind each of the following as 
   370â†’listed: 
   371â†’ 
   372â†’1. 
   373â†’No warning while the project is running. 
   374â†’2. 
   375â†’Do not insert the dongle key. 
   376â†’3. 
   377â†’Operate in Auto Mode. 
   378â†’4. 
   379â†’The warp target speeds up after running for the second time.

   385â†’ Subflow Node 
   386â†’When the number of Nodes in the flow becomes large, certain blocks of the project may be

   393â†’original page. The concept of modularization created with this method allows users to

   395â†’programming, it is recommended to use Subflow to simplify the whole flow, as shown in the

   490â†’11. AI Vision Node 
   491â†’The AI Vision Node comes as a Positioning node or an Inspection one. The positioning one goes by 
   492â†’object positioning, landmark positioning, servo type, and various positioning functions. The 
   493â†’Inspection node goes with variety of AOI identification functions. The appearances of both nodes in 
   494â†’the flow are associated with icons shown on the right. The Base icon on the right side indicates 
   495â†’which base records the snapping point of this Vision Job. The icon at the bottom right suggests the

     3â†’12. Communication and Display 
     4â†’Users can communicate with external devices through the TM AI Cobot-supported protocols (client-
     5â†’side), including Modbus, Network (socket), IO, etc. Users can easily use nodes for communication 
     6â†’settings when using the flow project or declare a device. The following will introduce communication 
     7â†’and Display through a flow project.  
     8â†’ 
     9â†’ Modbus 
    10â†’Modbus is a Master/Slave type communication protocol. Users can use Modbus Master to read or 
    11â†’write the parameters and save them in the robot register, such as position, posture and IO status. 
    12â†’Users can program with the obtained parameters or monitor the status of robot. TM AI Cobot 
    13â†’provides two protocol versions of Modbus: Modbus TCP and Modbus RTU for users to get data from 
    14â†’the external Modbus device or robot register. The external device, such as IPC or PLC, can also send 
    15â†’commends to the TM AI Cobot to get the related data as Master. 
    16â†’ 
    17â†’ 
    18â†’Figure 12 - 1: Robot Modbus Protocol 
    19â†’ 
    20â†’ 
    21â†’NOTE: 
    22â†’Master is also called Client; Slave is also called Server. 
    23â†’ 
    24â†’ Modbus System Hardware Structure 
    25â†’Modbus is divided into two protocol versions: Modbus TCP and Modbus RTU. Modbus TCP 
    26â†’uses RJ45 for communication, and Modbus RTU uses a serial port for communication. 
    27â†’

   212â†’ IO 
   213â†’TM AI Cobot provides users with both digital IO and analog IO. Digital IO controls two states by H 
   214â†’and L (High/Low). High denotes the output voltage of the Control Box, which is 24V. If Low, then 
   215â†’the output voltage is pulled to GND.  
   216â†’ 
   217â†’The Control Box configures 2 sets of digital IOs and 2 sets of analog IOs. Users can use the SET 
   218â†’node to give the analog IO specific voltage (-10V~10V) to complete the job in actual operation. 
   219â†’ 
   220â†’ User Defined IO 
   221â†’Using Self-Defined IO, users can trigger or read the button on the Robot Stick with external 
   222â†’devices through the IO ports on the Control Box. After the setting is complete, click Save in 
   223â†’the lower right corner to save the setting.

   302â†’ Display Node 
   303â†’The function of the Display Node is to display the specified variable or string on the screen

   307â†’change the color according to the results, and seven colors are supported: red, green, blue, yellow,

   344â†’13. Component 
   345â†’Component is an independent software package for the robot applications. For TM Plug & Play 
   346â†’supported items, users do not need to write additional programs or dig into technical documents of 
   347â†’both parties before integrating, but import the software package to use in TMflow directly. Place the 
   348â†’downloaded component in the folder named TM_Export in root directory of a USB drive labeled with 
   349â†’TMROBOT. Insert the USB drive into to Control Box and navigate to â‰، > System > Import/Export to 
   350â†’import the component onto the robot.

   355â†’as: TMROBOT:\TM_Export\RobotName\FileType\FileName\ . 
   356â†’The path names in bold font must follow an exact, case-sensitive naming convention 
   357â†’and the others can be named and renamed as users please.

     3â†’3. Safety Settings 
     4â†’ Overview 
     5â†’The following sections will introduce the safety settings interface of the TM AI Cobot, including the 
     6â†’Safety Permission Settings and Safety Setting. 
     7â†’ 
     8â†’ 
     9â†’ Safety Permission Settings 
    10â†’Users and administrators of TM AI Cobot must set appropriate account password permissions 
    11â†’before starting to use the TM AI Cobot, with proper arrangements for access to operator 
    12â†’permission for safety configuration. 
    13â†’ 
    14â†’When users have completed the startup and activation according to the previous chapter and 
    15â†’entered the TMflow interface with the default account password to get the control of the robot, 
    16â†’navigated to â‰، and clicked Configuration to enter the setting page, an option labeled Safety will 
    17â†’appear on this page. It is the safety settings operation area of the product with all the critical 
    18â†’settings for the robot. If settings are changed arbitrarily, it will cause danger during operation. For 
    19â†’proper permission settings, refer to 5.6.3.2 User & Permissionto create accounts for authorization 
    20â†’to access the safety- related setting permissions and grant permission to access Configuration to 
    21â†’set other accounts and group privileges to access Configuration to change the safety permission 
    22â†’settings. 
    23â†’ 
    24â†’ Safety Settings 
    25â†’The Safety Settings page comes with function buttons including Edit, New, Open, Default, 
    26â†’Save, and Apply, and settings of MODE, Speed & Force, Soft Axis, Safety IO, Safety 
    27â†’Tool, and Mounting Direction in the Configuration Tool. These functions deliver a Safety 
    28â†’Checksum at the top right to verify the system integrity. Changes in these functions renew the 
    29â†’Safety Checksum if applied. The version of the Safety System is at the bottom left of the screen. 
    30â†’There is also a timestamp of the last modified date and time at the bottom left of the screen. The 
    31â†’timestamp is updated and expressed according to ISO 8601 every time users click 
    32â†’the Apply button and confirm the new parameters. 
    33â†’NOTE: 
    34â†’Upgrading the software with previous versions of hardware will not upgrade the version 
    35â†’of the safety system.  
    36â†’
    37â†’[p.35]
    38â†’35 
    39â†’ 
    40â†’ 
    41â†’Figure 3 - 1: Safety Setting 
    42â†’ 
    43â†’ 
    44â†’ Function Buttons 
    45â†’This section will disclose the function buttons for the configuration of safety parameters. 
    46â†’ 
    47â†’ Edit 
    48â†’Click 
    49â†’ to start to configure the safety parameters and the safety passwords. After logging 
    50â†’in to the Configuration Tool with a valid password, users can configure the safety parameters 
    51â†’to apply to the safety module and change the passwords of the Configuration Tool and the 
    52â†’Robot Stick. To change either of the passwords, click the respective item to input the current 
    53â†’password, the new password, the new password again for confirmation, and make the 
    54â†’change. 
    55â†’ 
    56â†’NOTE: 
    57â†’Certain robot operations will not work, such as Recovering from ESTOP, until users stop 
    58â†’editing safety settings by either Saving or Canceling. 
    59â†’ 
    60â†’The default password of the Configuration Tool is 00000000, and users can change the 
    61â†’password with 4 to 8 alphanumeric characters. 
    62â†’ 
    63â†’NOTE: 
    64â†’The Safety Settings of Safety System 3.3 varies from that of Safety System 5.1. Please refer 
    65â†’to the Safety Manual of the respective Safety System for details. 
    66â†’
    67â†’[p.36]
    68â†’36 
    69â†’The default password of the Robot Stick isï¼‹ï¼چï¼‹ï¼‹ï¼چ, and users can replace the password 
    70â†’with the combination of 4~8 keystrokes of + and -. 
    71â†’ 
    72â†’NOTE: 
    73â†’There is no Robot Stick password configuration in HW3.2. The password is fixed to + - + + -. 
    74â†’ 
    75â†’ 
    76â†’WARNING: 
    77â†’Users should consider the strength and the complexity of the password to prevent 
    78â†’unauthorized decryption. It is the usersâ€™ responsibility to ensure the password security 
    79â†’and the correctness of safety configuration in advance. 
    80â†’ 
    81â†’ New 
    82â†’Click 
    83â†’ to create a new configuration file with all the safety parameters set to the defaults 
    84â†’by different robot model types. Users can apply these settings directly or change any safety 
    85â†’parameter according to the risk assessment. Immediately using the default turns all safety 
    86â†’settings back to the default. 
    87â†’ 
    88â†’ Open 
    89â†’Click 
    90â†’ to open the existing configuration file. All the existing configuration files are 
    91â†’present with their Safety Checksums. Users can delete the existing configuration files by 
    92â†’clicking 
    93â†’. The name of currently opened configuration file shows at the bottom left of the 
    94â†’screen. 
    95â†’ 
    96â†’ Default 
    97â†’Click 
    98â†’ to set values for the safety parameters in current settings page to default values by 
    99â†’different robot model types. 
   100â†’ 
   101â†’ Save 
   102â†’Click the save icon to save the safety parameter values in the setting page into a 
   103â†’configuration file. The configuration file naming supports Latin alphabet letters in upper 
   104â†’case and lower case (A-Z, a-z), Arabic digits (0-9), and the character _. The configuration file 
   105â†’saves with its Safety Checksum. 
   106â†’ 
   107â†’ Apply 
   108â†’Click the apply icon to apply the safety parameter values in the setting page to the safety 
   109â†’
   110â†’[p.37]
   111â†’37 
   112â†’module. After clicking, all the safety parameter information will present in a sheet for users 
   113â†’to confirm whether to apply these values. If confirming YES by checking the checkbox at the 
   114â†’bottom of the sheet, click Apply to apply these values to the safety module. After applying, a 
   115â†’pop-out window will be displayed for users to save the sheet to an external flash drive. Note 
   116â†’that the flash drive must be labeled TMROBOT. 
   117â†’ 
   118â†’ MODE 
   119â†’On this page, users can configure either the T1 Setting or the TCH Setting to have the 
   120â†’MANUAL MODE map to either T1 MODE or TCH MODE (Teaching MODE). Users can also 
   121â†’configure the Enabling Switch Functions by the risk assessment and the integration. For 
   122â†’details of these MODEs, refer to the relevant contents in the Safety Manual. 
   123â†’ 
   124â†’ 
   125â†’NOTE: 
   126â†’There is no MANUAL MODE mapping in HW3.2. HW3.2 provides only MANUAL MODE 
   127â†’and AUTO MODE. Please refer to the Safety Manual of the respective Safety System 
   128â†’for details. 
   129â†’ 
   130â†’ 
   131â†’DANGER: 
   132â†’When selecting to suspend Enabling Switch, the triggering effect of Enabling Switch 
   133â†’will be disabled. Users must properly configure the speed limit, power and force limit, 
   134â†’axis, and space limit by the risk assessment. Users must take responsibility and 
   135â†’ensure the robot will have no chance of contacting the body regions of users. 
   136â†’Operating the product without proper evaluation on risks, testing, and carefully 
   137â†’reading the productâ€™s operating manual, this product would dump or pinch to the 
   138â†’users, even causing life-threatening dangers. Failure to perform a proper risk 
   139â†’assessment, the configuration of safety limits, or comply with the safety manuals may 
   140â†’increase the risk of injury or death. 
   141â†’ 
   142â†’ Speed & Force 
   143â†’On this page, users can set the limit values of Speed Limit functions and Force and Torque 
   144â†’Limit functions in either Performance Safety Settings or Human-Machine Safety Settings. 
   145â†’ 
   146â†’ 
   147â†’NOTE: 
   148â†’For the settings of Speed & Force in HW3.2, please refer to SF4, SF6, SF7, and SF8 in the 
   149â†’Safety Manual of the respective Safety System for details. 
   150â†’ 
   151â†’ General 
   152â†’
   153â†’[p.38]
   154â†’38 
   155â†’ïپ¬ 
   156â†’Users can set the limit values of Hand Guide TCP speed in either T1 MODE or TCH 
   157â†’MODE by configurations of the MODE setting. The Hand Guide TCP speed limit 
   158â†’functions only during hand guiding. If configured the MANUAL MODE to T1 MODE, 
   159â†’the Hand Guide speed limit follows the limit value of the T1 Hand Guide speed limit. 
   160â†’Alternatively, The Hand Guide speed limit follows the limit value of the TCH Hand 
   161â†’Guide speed limit in TCH MODE. 
   162â†’ïپ¬ 
   163â†’Users can set the value of the End-Point Reduced Speed Limit. This speed limit 
   164â†’functions only in T1 MODE. 
   165â†’ïپ¬ 
   166â†’Users can set different access to configurations of Force and Torque Limit functions 
   167â†’monitoring applications of effective at all times, effective only when triggering 
   168â†’HMSS, and no effect at all times. 
   169â†’ïپ¬ 
   170â†’Users can set Monitored Criteria Switching Time for beginning to Human-Machine 
   171â†’Safety Settings after triggered. 
   172â†’ïپ¬ 
   173â†’Before performing the operations or the settings, read and follow the instructions in 
   174â†’the Safety Manual for the associated physical explanation and the definitions of 
   175â†’safety functions and the precautions. 
   176â†’ïپ¬ 
   177â†’Users can refer to Appendix B: Tables of Safety Parameter Upper and Lower Bounds 
   178â†’for the limit values applicable to each model. 
   179â†’ïپ¬ 
   180â†’The default limit value of the stop criteria aims to provide a relatively safer working 
   181â†’capability for the robot. Users can set a higher upper limit. If users want to reach 
   182â†’100% project override speed without triggering these stop criteria, set the limit 
   183â†’value to the upper limit for each input slot. Since the robot capability is associated 
   184â†’with the pose, motion, TCP length, and payload, without these stop criteria, the 
   185â†’robot is protected with the maximum allowable torque of each joint and stop. In 
   186â†’addition, regarding the robot lifespan, refer to the Hardware Installation Manual of 
   187â†’the respective model for the value of rated torque and the limit for repeated peak 
   188â†’torque of each joint. 
   189â†’ 
   190â†’ 
   191â†’DANGER: 
   192â†’Users can configure the Force & Torque Limit function through risk assessments and 
   193â†’application scenarios, and users must take responsibility and ensure the robot will 
   194â†’have no chance of contacting the body regions of users. Operating the product 
   195â†’without proper evaluation of risks, testing, and carefully reading its operating manual 
   196â†’may result in this product dumping or pinching users that cause even life-threatening 
   197â†’dangers. Failure to perform a proper risk assessment or the configuration of safety 
   198â†’limits or to comply with the safety manuals may increase the risk of injury or death. 
   199â†’
   200â†’[p.39]
   201â†’39 
   202â†’ 
   203â†’ïپ¬ 
   204â†’Robot in the fenceless workspace: the safety module will monitor the Force & 
   205â†’Torque Limit from start to finish. 
   206â†’ïپ¬ 
   207â†’Only activating under Human-Machine Safety: the safety module will monitor 
   208â†’the Force & Torque Limit only while activating Human-Machine Safety. 
   209â†’ïپ¬ 
   210â†’Robot in the fence: the safety module will not monitor the Force & Torque Limit 
   211â†’once selected. Users are obligated to make sure the robot will have no chance to 
   212â†’be in contact with their body regions. 
   213â†’ 
   214â†’For particular hardware versions with Mute Joint Torque Monitoring and TCP 
   215â†’Force Limit when User Connected External Safeguard Input Signal at LOW, 
   216â†’enabling this item will suspend Force & Torque Limit function always when User 
   217â†’Connected External Safeguard Input Signal is LOW. 
   218â†’ 
   219â†’ 
   220â†’NOTE: 
   221â†’1. 
   222â†’While using with Hand Guide, users will feel the resistance feedback if the pull 
   223â†’speed by hand reaches the Hand Guide TCP speed limit. Under such 
   224â†’circumstances, lower the strength to pull to keep from triggering the protective 
   225â†’stop. 
   226â†’2. 
   227â†’The Monitored Criteria Switching Time is a configurable time delay after 
   228â†’triggering the Human-Machine Safety Settings function to begin to monitor the 
   229â†’speed and the force limit settings to have the robot decrease the speed to the 
   230â†’Human-Machine Safety Settings. It comes with a deceleration time parameter in 
   231â†’Motion Settings that users can configure to prevent force/torque-related safety 
   232â†’functions from triggering by the dramatic slowdown from high speed. Users 
   233â†’should take these timings into considerations regarding the risk assessment of 
   234â†’applications and setting the distance of the safeguard devices properly. 
   235â†’ 
   236â†’ Performance Safety 
   237â†’ïپ¬ 
   238â†’Users can set the speed limit values of safety tools and joints, the force limit values 
   239â†’of TCP and elbow, and the torque limit values of the joints. These limits function 
   240â†’under both AUTO MODE and MANUAL MODE. Before performing the operations or 
   241â†’the settings, read and follow the instructions in the Safety Manual for the associated 
   242â†’physical explanation and the definitions of safety functions and the precautions. 
   243â†’Users can refer to Appendix B: Tables of Safety Parameter Upper and Lower Bounds 
   244â†’for the limit values applicable to each model. 
   245â†’ 
   246â†’ Human-Machine Safety 
   247â†’ïپ¬ 
   248â†’Once triggered by any Human-Machine Safety Settings function, the robot will run 
   249â†’at a slower speed and a lower joint torque stop criteria. At this time, a purple light 
   250â†’will be added to the Indication Light Ring for users to distinguish whether the robot 
   251â†’
   252â†’[p.40]
   253â†’40 
   254â†’is switching into Human-Machine Safety Settings. 
   255â†’ïپ¬ 
   256â†’Users can set the speed limit value for safety tools and joints, the force limit value of 
   257â†’TCP and elbow, and the torque limit values of joints for Human-Machine Safety 
   258â†’Settings. Users can refer to Appendix B: Tables of Safety Parameter Upper and 
   259â†’Lower Bounds for limit values applicable to each model. 
   260â†’ 
   261â†’ 
   262â†’DANGER: 
   263â†’Note that the functions described in this section are to assist users in setting the 
   264â†’Human-Machine safety parameters and settings more conveniently only. Users should 
   265â†’still perform a complete risk assessment according to the robot use environment and 
   266â†’conditions before using the robot. TM AI Cobot specifies the following potential 
   267â†’residual risks: There is a risk that causes the robot to hit a human body at full speed 
   268â†’due to improper use of safety settings or by running incorrect projects. 
   269â†’ 
   270â†’ 
   271â†’DANGER: 
   272â†’When using the Compliance function in TMflow, speed is not under control 
   273â†’in Human-Machine Safety Settings. The robot will still run the Compliance function 
   274â†’by the force users set. If you want to use the Compliance function with Human-
   275â†’Machine Safety Setting, complete a full risk assessment and set appropriate force 
   276â†’values. 
   277â†’ 
   278â†’ïپ¬ 
   279â†’This function also provides a Quick Setting by choosing body contact 
   280â†’regions. Users can set the human body region that may be in contact with the robot 
   281â†’in the collaborative workspace according to the requirements. The calculation 
   282â†’result includes the limit value of the speed limit of the safety tool. Users must check 
   283â†’the confirmation field at the right side before confirming the area where the 
   284â†’external device of the robot may be contacting the human body larger than or equal 
   285â†’to the area confirmation value. This feature is designed for users to quickly set up 
   286â†’an initial robot application in the collaborative workspace following the 
   287â†’biomechanical limits of each body region listed in ISO/TS 15066. Users should still 
   288â†’perform a risk assessment on real applications before deployment. Users should 
   289â†’take responsibility for the human body region not listed in this graph and ensure 
   290â†’the robot does not have any chance of contacting any vulnerable body region such 
   291â†’as the spine and hindbrain. 
   292â†’ïپ¬ 
   293â†’For the detailed test of initial parameters, refer to the relevant contents in the 
   294â†’Safety Manual.

     3â†’14. Force Related 
     4â†’This chapter will introduce the force with the TM AI Cobot to conduct more applications such as 
     5â†’collision testing, object assembly, object searching, polishing, deburring, inserting, etc. The flow 
     6â†’project goes with the F/T sensor device, the touch stop node, the smart-insert node, and the force 
     7â†’control node to achieve the force operations. The following will introduce Force Related nodes 
     8â†’through the flow project.  
     9â†’ 
    10â†’ 
    11â†’NOTE: 
    12â†’Users can set the target force for the robot to operate based on the reference 
    13â†’coordinate to. However, the reading of F/T graph and parameterized objects is the 
    14â†’force sensed by the F/T sensor concerning the reference coordinate in the nodes. 
    15â†’According to the law of action and reaction, the sensor reading will equal to a 
    16â†’negative value of the target force for the robot to operate. 
    17â†’ 
    18â†’ Compliance node 
    19â†’The Compliance node can set the force limit on the robot movement along a single axis. This 
    20â†’setting can be used for various applications of collision testing, object assembly, and object 
    21â†’searching. Users can determine the direction of robot motion based on the Tool or the Base. 
    22â†’

    38â†’directions of the compliance. 
    39â†’Impedance to set Stiffness to have the robot hold its current position but also allow 
    40â†’complying with external forces to reduce torque on the joints. It is for use, for example, 
    41â†’when mounting the robot on a moving base, such as an AMR, experiences bouncing 
    42â†’motions that cause the torque on the joints.

   112â†’The compliance settings of the Node comes with: Single Axis, Teach, and Advanced. Refer to the

   114â†’to teach with line direction or rotation direction. Users can use the two points of teaching to

   116â†’relevant parameters, users can specify the speed of motion and other additional stop conditions in

   164â†’NOTE: 
   165â†’Users can use Advanced In Compliance Setting as a way to edit the force and the 
   166â†’torque on each axis in compliance motions without force sensor and to function 
   167â†’more practically to setup the application strategy. Users can choose axes as rigid or 
   168â†’compliant and decide the target force. Advanced is useful for applications with

   183â†’ F/T Sensor 
   184â†’TM AI Cobot integrates F/T sensors from a variety of brands into TMflow for users to configure and 
   185â†’utilize instantly. At the top left, users can click Project function > F/T Sensor to select and 
   186â†’configure F/T sensors as shown below. 
   187â†’ 
   188â†’

   194â†’After configuring the F/T Sensor communication and position settings, users can use the 
   195â†’configured F/T sensor.  
   196â†’ 
   197â†’ Communication Setting  
   198â†’Users can configure the model numbers and the communication ports of the TM AI Cobot 
   199â†’supported F/T sensors in the communication setting of F/T Sensor.  
   200â†’ 
   201â†’To configure the communication setting, follow the steps. 
   202â†’1. 
   203â†’Click the icon of F/T sensor, and click Add Device. 
   204â†’2. 
   205â†’Select Communication Setting. 
   206â†’3. 
   207â†’Fill a desired name in the field of Device Name. 
   208â†’4. 
   209â†’Select Vendor/Model and Com port of the F/T sensor in the respective dropdowns. 
   210â†’5. 
   211â†’Click OK when done. 
   212â†’ 
   213â†’ 
   214â†’NOTE: 
   215â†’Users can fill self-defined names in field of Device Name, select vendor/Model of 
   216â†’the installed F/T sensor as well as Com port that the installed F/T sensor plugged 
   217â†’in, and click OK to complete the setting. Baud Rate, Data Bit, Stop Bit, and Parity 
   218â†’are for confirmations only and not configurable by users. 
   219â†’ 
   220â†’Once the setting is done, users are able to view the configured F/T sensor and values of axes 
   221â†’on each direction sensed by the F/T sensor listed in F/T sensor as shown.

   236â†’in this version.

   238â†’ Position Setting  
   239â†’Users can define the position of the F/T sensor to the TM AI Cobot flange in Position Setting 
   240â†’to convert the coordinates that the F/T sensor measured to the coordinates of the robot 
   241â†’flange. The calculation method of the F/T sensor position is as shown in the figure below. 
   242â†’Users have to measure the values of X, Y, and Z of the F/T sensor to the TM AI Cobot flange 
   243â†’and confirm the rotation angle RX, RY, and RZ based on the angle the F/T sensor installed 
   244â†’onto the TM AI Cobot flange.

   250â†’flange, users can fill the values of the positions and the angles in Position Setting as shown

   262â†’ Import/Export Settings of F/T Sensor  
   263â†’TMflow supports import/export settings of F/T Sensor. Users can export the configured F/T 
   264â†’sensor settings and import to the other TM AI Cobots. All users have to do is to export the 
   265â†’projects with F/T sensor settings configured and the settings relative to the F/T sensor will be 
   266â†’exported along. Refer to 5.6.2.1 Import/Export for details 
   267â†’

   269â†’Once the settings of the F/T sensor are configured, users can observe lines of forces and 
   270â†’torques of the F/T sensor in the charts as shown below. Users can also view the fluctuations 
   271â†’of forces and torques in the running task while editing in TMflow. To show the charts, click

   277â†’before the force or the torque to show the graph on the charts. Check Auto Scaling to have 
   278â†’the system scale the detected values automatically or set the maximum and the minimum 
   279â†’manually.

300â†’ Touch Stop Node 
   301â†’The Touch Stop node comes with three function types: Compliance, Line, and Force Sensor. 
   302â†’ 
   303â†’ Function Type: Compliance 
   304â†’Capable of setting the force limit when the robot moves along a single Base, this setting can 
   305â†’be used for various applications of object searching, creating a new Base, and recording the 
   306â†’current coordinate value of triggering Touch Stop. 
   307â†’ 
   308â†’ 
   309â†’Figure 14 - 12: Touch Stop-Compliance Settings 
   310â†’ 
   311â†’ïپ¬ 
   312â†’Choose Base: Select Tool or Current Base, and click OK to have the robot move 
   313â†’accordingly.  
   314â†’ïپ¬ 
   315â†’Setting: Select Single Axis to define the direction (axis), distance, target force/Torque, 
   316â†’speed of the compliance, Teach to use manual teaching method, or Advanced to define 
   317â†’force/torque, distance limit, and target speed for applicable directions of compliance. 
   318â†’ïپ¬ 
   319â†’Stop Criteria: 
   320â†’ïپ® 
   321â†’Over: Time: This node will be released if the set time is reached before or while 
   322â†’running the job.

   392â†’ Function Type: Line 
   393â†’This function is designed to set the robot's line movement along a Single Axis, or direction 
   394â†’Teach, and works with the external signals to stop the robot motion. This setting can be 
   395â†’used for sensors on external tools to record the position. Users can determine the direction 
   396â†’of robot motion based on the Tool or the base.

   406â†’teaching. Check the box next to Link to project speed to align the speed with the 
   407â†’project speed.

   432â†’Braking distance: Set the distance available from 0 to 125 مژœ for the robot putting 
   433â†’the brake on digital input or analog input. The maximum distance available is 125
   434â†’مژœ. An input value larger than 125 will give an error message.

   437â†’in kilograms. 
   438â†’ïپ¬

   440â†’Triggered position and enter the name in the field below to record the robot position, 
   441â†’as a dynamic point, at the time when the robot puts on the brake. The dynamic point 
   442â†’will be in Point Manager when you have:

   449â†’speed when this button is pressed.

   451â†’ Touch Stop Function Type: Force Sensor 
   452â†’Of the three function types in a Touch Stop node, Force Sensor is the only one that uses the 
   453â†’force sensor to proceed with the force touch stop measurement. Once configured, users can 
   454â†’select Force Sensor to assign the desired device as shown below, and use the device to 
   455â†’measure the sensed force along the named directions.  
   456â†’ 
   457â†’
   458â†’[p.214]
   459â†’214 
   460â†’ 
   461â†’Figure 14 - 14: Touch Stop-Force Sensor 
   462â†’ 
   463â†’To select a configured force sensor, follow these steps. 
   464â†’1. 
   465â†’Click the pencil icon of the Touch Stop node. 
   466â†’2. 
   467â†’Click the Force sensor button in Function Type. 
   468â†’3. 
   469â†’Click the box next to Choose device to select from the list of the configured force 
   470â†’sensors. 
   471â†’4. 
   472â†’Click OK when done. 
   473â†’ 
   474â†’ïپ¬ 
   475â†’Set to zero for force sensor before execution: For more precise measured values 
   476â†’while running force tasks, users can check this feature before running force tasks. This 
   477â†’feature sets zero to every current axis value of the force sensor, so the returned force 
   478â†’values reflect the real force in the force tasks. During the force sensor zero-out process, 
   479â†’ensure that the force sensor is not in contact with any object to avoid additional 
   480â†’external forces that may affect the measurement results of the force sensor. 
   481â†’ïپ¬ 
   482â†’Choose Base: Select Tool or Current Base, and click OK to have the robot move 
   483â†’accordingly. 
   484â†’ïپ¬ 
   485â†’Setting: Set the robot to move along the direction of a single axis, teach using manual 
   486â†’teaching. 
   487â†’ïپ® 
   488â†’Stop Criteria: 
   489â†’ïپ® 
   490â†’Over: Time: Set the length of time to stop and release this node 
   491â†’ïپ® 
   492â†’Over: F/T: There are three type of force reached to choose: Force, F3D, and T3D as 
   493â†’shown below. 
   494â†’ 
   495â†’
   496â†’[p.215]
   497â†’215 
   498â†’ 
   499â†’Figure 14 - 15: Force Sensor 
   500â†’ 
   501â†’ 
   502â†’NOTE: 
   503â†’Force will change the measurement unit based on usersâ€™ setting in Single Axis. In 
   504â†’other words, if users set Direction to Rx, Ry, or Rz, the measurement unit will 
   505â†’change from N to Nm. Force/Torque monitors the forces/torques along the named 
   506â†’directions. Once it reaches the preset values of the forces/torques, the robot stops 
   507â†’the detection movement to proceed with the next assigned movement, namely 
   508â†’that forces/torques along the other directions but not the named directions will not 
   509â†’fulfill the stop criteria of the Touch Stop node while the project is in progress. 
   510â†’ 
   511â†’ïپ® 
   512â†’Receive: Var: Set rules to judge from the outcomes of variables in expressions 
   513â†’ïپ® 
   514â†’Receive: DI: Set a digital Input signal to release this node once a specific DI is 
   515â†’triggered 
   516â†’ïپ® 
   517â†’Receive: AI: Set an analog input signal that, when met, the node is released. 
   518â†’ïپ® 
   519â†’Others: 
   520â†’ïپ® 
   521â†’Output Variable: An integer to show the result of the Compliance, meaning which 
   522â†’criteria are being triggered in the first place, and should have the following 
   523â†’possibilities: 
   524â†’2 
   525â†’Timeout 
   526â†’3 
   527â†’Distance Reach 
   528â†’6 
   529â†’ERROR (including TCP speed over limit, incorrect timing of DI triggered, etc.) 
   530â†’201 Digital Input 
   531â†’202 Analog Input 
   532â†’203 Variable 
   533â†’204 Force Satisfied 
   534â†’
   535â†’[p.216]
   536â†’216 
   537â†’ïپ® 
   538â†’Change payload to: If equipped, set the weight of the device at the end of the robot 
   539â†’in kilograms. 
   540â†’ 
   541â†’ 
   542â†’NOTE: 
   543â†’Force and torque will be obtained when the tool of the robot collides with 
   544â†’environment. The reaction force/torque will also operate at robot. If the robot 
   545â†’switches to position control, the robot might generate a safety alarm due to 
   546â†’reaction force/torque. In order to avoid the error safety alarm, the Touch Stop 
   547â†’node will turn control mode to Compliance when force/torque reaches usersâ€™ 
   548â†’stop criteria. Users can set payload which is the weight from the robot flange to the 
   549â†’end of the tool for having better Compliance operation. 
   550â†’ 
   551â†’ïپ¬ 
   552â†’Record Stopping Position on POINT: Select from the Stopping position or the 
   553â†’Triggered position and enter the name in the field below to record the robot position,

   567â†’ 
   568â†’ Smart Insert Node 
   569â†’The Smart Insert Node allows the robot to perform assembly/pushing jobs. The smart design 
   570â†’enables difficult object assembly/pushing jobs to be completed through simple and quick setting. 
   571â†’The inserting action of Smart Insert Node can be divided into three steps: Approaching, 
   572â†’Searching, and Pushing. This Node needs to be worked with the cooperating force sensor in TM 
   573â†’Plug & Play for use. The following describes the three steps of pushing. 
   574â†’ 
   575â†’

   578â†’of the force sensor, or it will result in misjudgment of the contact force. If it is not 
   579â†’possible to set so, instead use the force control node to measure the contact force of the 
   580â†’inserting object rather than use the smart insert node.

   586â†’ Approaching principle description 
   587â†’Before using the Smart Insert node, users should place the inserting object as close to 
   588â†’the assembly as possible. In the Approaching step, the robot will move in the z axis 
   589â†’direction of the Tool Coordinate until the force sensor detects 5 Newtons (N). 
   590â†’ 
   591â†’ 
   592â†’Figure 14 - 16: Approaching Principle 
   593â†’ 
   594â†’(a) Try to position the robot as close to the socket as possible 
   595â†’(b) When the contact force is 5 Newtons, the robot ends Approaching 
   596â†’
